sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after RIGHT. Let me compare the before and after frames to see what moved. The green object was at [49-51, 32-35] before. Let me check if it moved right, or if something else changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after RIGHT action
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find the green object in both frames
def find_green(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'N':
            return n
    return None

before_green = find_green(before_seg)
after_green = find_green(after_seg)

print("Before green:", before_green['boundary'] if before_green else "None")
print("After green:", after_green['boundary'] if after_green else "None")

# Check all objects for changes
print("\nBefore objects:")
for n in before_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'w' and n['color'] != 'P':  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nAfter objects:")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'w' and n['color'] != 'P':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

Pink HUD at bottom.", "goal_model": "Unknown", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT caused a board change"], "open_questions": ["What moved when RIGHT was pressed?", "What is the player?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Check what changed after RIGHT, then try more movements", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze what changed:
Before RIGHT:
- Green (N) object: boundary [[49, 32], [49, 35], [51, 35], [51, 32]] - a 3x4 object at rows 49-51, cols 32-35
- White (W) object: boundary [[48, 32], [48, 35]] - a 1x4 bar at row 48, cols 32-35
After RIGHT:
- Green (N) object: boundary [[48, 36], [48, 38], [51, 38], [51, 36]] - now at rows 48-51, cols 36-38 (moved right by ~4 cols, and up by 1 row)
- White (W) object: boundary [[48, 39], [51, 39]] - now a vertical bar at col 39, rows 48-51
Wait, this is interesting. The green object moved from cols 32-35 to cols 36-38, and the white object changed from horizontal bar to vertical bar. It seems like the green object is the player and it moved right. But the movement seems to have also affected the white bar.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Green went from [49-51, 32-35] to [48-51, 36-38] - moved right 4 columns and up 1 row
- White went from [48, 32-35] (horizontal) to [48-51, 39] (vertical)
The white bar seems to have transformed. It was above the green object, and after moving right, it became a vertical bar to the right. This might be a trail or path indicator.
Let me think about this differently. The green object is the player. When it moves RIGHT, it moves 4 pixels to the right. The white bar seems to mark where it was before (the previous position).
Let me try more movements to understand the pattern. Let me try RIGHT again to see if it moves another 4 pixels, or try UP/DOWN/LEFT.
Actually, let me reconsider. The green object moved from cols 32-35 to 36-38. That's a shift of +4 in column. The white bar was at cols 32-35 (where green was) and is now at col 39 (to the right of green's new position). 
Wait - the white bar was at row 48, cols 32-35 before. After, it's at rows 48-51, col 39. So it changed from a horizontal bar above the green object to a vertical bar to the right of the new green position.
This is confusing. Let me just try more movements and see the pattern. Let me try moving RIGHT again, then UP, DOWN, LEFT to understand the movement mechanics.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The green (N) object moved from [49-51, 32-35] to [48-51, 36-38] after RIGHT
# The white (W) bar changed from horizontal [48, 32-35] to vertical [48-51, 39]
# Green appears to be the player, moving 4 pixels right
# White bar might be a trail/marker

# Let me try moving in different directions to understand movement
# First try UP
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
